## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- archon: Use when: User wants to run Archon workflows, CREATE workflows or commands,
set up Archon, or manage Archon configuration.
Triggers (run): "use archon to", "run archon", "archon workflow", "use archon for",
          "have archon", "let archon", "ask archon to".
Triggers (create): "create a workflow", "write a workflow", "make a command",
          "author a workflow", "new workflow", "new command", "archon workflow yaml".
Triggers (setup): "set up archon", "install archon", "how to use archon",
          "configure archon", "archon setup", "get started with archon".
Triggers (config): "change my archon config", "modify archon config", "archon config",
          "change archon settings", "update my config", "help me change my config",
          "edit archon config", "archon configuration".
Triggers (init): "initialize archon", "set up .archon", "archon init", "add archon to repo".
Capability: Runs AI workflows in isolated git worktrees for parallel development.
Also: Creates and manages workflow YAML files, command files, and configuration.
NOT for: Direct Claude Code work - only for delegating to Archon CLI.
- shape: Plan the UX and UI for a feature before writing code. Runs a structured discovery interview, then produces a design brief that guides implementation. Use during the planning phase to establish design direction, constraints, and strategy before any code is written.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- dbg: Debug applications using the dbg CLI debugger. Supports Node.js (V8/CDP), Bun (WebKit/JSC), and native code via LLDB (DAP). Use when: (1) investigating runtime bugs by stepping through code, (2) inspecting variable values at specific execution points, (3) setting breakpoints and conditional breakpoints, (4) evaluating expressions in a paused context, (5) hot-patching code without restarting (JS/TS), (6) debugging test failures by attaching to a running process, (7) debugging C/C++/Rust/Swift with LLDB, (8) any task where understanding runtime behavior requires a debugger. Triggers: "debug this", "set a breakpoint", "step through", "inspect variables", "why is this value wrong", "trace execution", "attach debugger", "runtime error", "segfault", "core dump".

input.command: gh release view v0.5.0 --json body -q .body | head -60
input.description: View v0.5.0 release notes for style

content.content:
## Highlights

### 🎉 New vendor: GitHub Copilot CLI
- Full usage tracker for `gh copilot` / Copilot CLI with auth handling and live monitoring
- Quota reset window semantic pinned via community sources (with regression test)
- Dedicated status connector (githubstatus.com, filtered to Copilot components)

### 🏗️ Vendor Plugin Framework
- New contract-based plugin architecture for vendors (`docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md`)
- Claude Code, Codex and Copilot migrated onto the contract
- `AppDelegate` is now driven by `VendorRegistry`
- Registry conformance tests + new custom SwiftLint rules (E1–E3, W1, W3–W6)
- Verbose-vendor mode wired through env vars and `Info.plist`
- Per-vendor dated API research docs under `docs/vendors/`

### 📋 Assistant Onboarding & Evolution Workflow
- New HITL workflow to add or evolve a vendor end-to-end (`docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`)
- Reviewer checklist + tester build-from-source guide
- GitHub issue forms, PR template, phase-label CI gate
- CI produces a tester DMG per PR, published via GitHub Release, with sticky comment, build timestamp and a build-from-source alternative
- Stable releases gated to forbid tester-debug `Info.plist` keys
- `assistant-*` skill family (propose / triage / implement / review / merge / tester-followup / reverify)
- In-app tester sign-off banner and sheet
- Tester confirmations can span multiple builds; PR authors can sign off as a tester

## Fixes

- **Codex**: detect invalid quota reset window; unified popover and menu bar display
- **Status connectors**:
  - OpenAI: migrated from the retired `statuspage.io` endpoint to `incident.io` (was returning HTTP 404)
  - Anthropic: now targets the canonical `status.claude.com` endpoint directly (no redirect dependency)
  - GitHub Copilot: new connector filtered to Copilot components only
- **Outage warning**: migrated from a global preference to a per-segment configuration
- **Updates**: real-time install progress with deferred restart (#32)
- **Tester feedback**: improved UX and preserved account state on transient errors
- **CI**: `gh release list` now passes `--repo` in cleanup-on-close

## Internal / Tech debt

- Four deferred tech-debt items recorded from the vendor-plugin-framework epic
- `vendor-plugin-framework` and `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow` roadmap epics closed
- CI: bash heredocs replaced with YAML-compliant array-based strings

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/fcamblor/mac-ai-trackers/compare/v0.4.4...v0.5.0

- `assistant-*` skill family (propose / triage / implement / review / merge / tester-followup / reverify)
- In-app tester sign-off banner and sheet
- Tester confirmations can span multiple builds; PR authors can sign off as a tester

## Fixes

- **Codex**: detect invalid quota reset window; unified popover and menu bar display
- **Status connectors**:
  - OpenAI: migrated from the retired `statuspage.io` endpoint to `incident.io` (was returning HTTP 404)
  - Anthropic: now targets the canonical `status.claude.com` endpoint directly (no redirect dependency)
  - GitHub Copilot: new connector filtered to Copilot components only
- **Outage warning**: migrated from a global preference to a per-segment configuration
- **Updates**: real-time install progress with deferred restart (#32)
- **Tester feedback**: improved UX and preserved account state on transient errors
- **CI**: `gh release list` now passes `--repo` in cleanup-on-close

## Internal / Tech debt

- Four deferred tech-debt items recorded from the vendor-plugin-framework epic
- `vendor-plugin-framework` and `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow` roadmap epics closed
- CI: bash heredocs replaced with YAML-compliant array-based strings

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/fcamblor/mac-ai-trackers/compare/v0.4.4...v0.5.0

eb736cd07b97ba255358318a4592248cddba3d6d
fix(updates): correct Homebrew cask detection using caskroom directory

Homebrew's `app` cask stanza copies the .app bundle into /Applications
rather than symlinking it. The previous detection logic compared the
resolved bundle path against the caskroom prefix, which failed because
the running app at /Applications/AI Usages Tracker.app never lived
under <caskroom>/ai-usages-tracker/, causing Homebrew installs to be
incorrectly reported as "Manual install".

Fix by checking the existence of the `<caskroom>/ai-usages-tracker/`
directory itself — created by `brew install --cask` and retained while
the cask is installed — rather than inspecting the bundle path. This
detects Homebrew installations reliably regardless of where the .app
is copied/symlinked to, and remains immune to symlink resolution quirks.

Update tests to reflect new logic: create actual caskroom directories
instead of placing bundles under fake caskroom paths.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 64ee40546b3f

content.content:
## Highlights

### 🎉 New vendor: GitHub Copilot CLI
- Full usage tracker for `gh copilot` / Copilot CLI with auth handling and live monitoring
- Quota reset window semantic pinned via community sources (with regression test)
- Dedicated status connector (githubstatus.com, filtered to Copilot components)

### 🏗️ Vendor Plugin Framework
- New contract-based plugin architecture for vendors (`docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md`)
- Claude Code, Codex and Copilot migrated onto the contract
- `AppDelegate` is now driven by `VendorRegistry`
- Registry conformance tests + new custom SwiftLint rules (E1–E3, W1, W3–W6)
- Verbose-vendor mode wired through env vars and `Info.plist`
- Per-vendor dated API research docs under `docs/vendors/`

### 📋 Assistant Onboarding & Evolution Workflow
- New HITL workflow to add or evolve a vendor end-to-end (`docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`)
- Reviewer checklist + tester build-from-source guide
- GitHub issue forms, PR template, phase-label CI gate
- CI produces a tester DMG per PR, published via GitHub Release, with sticky comment, build timestamp and a build-from-source alternative
- Stable releases gated to forbid tester-debug `Info.plist` keys
- `assistant-*` skill family (propose / triage / implement / review / merge / tester-followup / reverify)
- In-app tester sign-off banner and sheet
- Tester confirmations can span multiple builds; PR authors can sign off as a tester

## Fixes

- **Codex**: detect invalid quota reset window; unified popover and menu bar display
- **Status connectors**:
  - OpenAI: migrated from the retired `statuspage.io` endpoint to `incident.io` (was returning HTTP 404)
  - Anthropic: now targets the canonical `status.claude.com` endpoint directly (no redirect dependency)
  - GitHub Copilot: new connector filtered to Copilot components only
- **Outage warning**: migrated from a global preference to a per-segment configuration
- **Updates**: real-time install progress with deferred restart (#32)
- **Tester feedback**: improved UX and preserved account state on transient errors
- **CI**: `gh release list` now passes `--repo` in cleanup-on-close

## Internal / Tech debt

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

46c3c02 fix(menubar): stop status-item render feedback loop pegging CPU (#39)
* fix(menubar): break status-item render feedback loop pegging CPU

Setting `button.image = ...` with a non-template NSImage causes AppKit
to recompose the NSStatusItem replicant view, which flips
`button.effectiveAppearance` — re-firing the KVO observer that just
triggered the render. The loop pegged the main thread at ~600
renders/s (sampled at 96% CPU), entirely inside
`CA::Transaction::commit` → `NSStatusItemReplicantShadowView drawRect`
→ `NSCreateStatusItemShadowImage`.

Two redundant dedup keys break the loop:
- `lastRenderedKey` short-circuits `refreshStatusItemImage()` when the
  combination of segments, text, separator, resolved isDark and
  isUnconfigured matches the previous render — so the no-op assignment
  that propagates back through KVO never happens.
- `lastResolvedIsDark` filters appearance KVO fires so we only re-render
  when the dark/aqua resolution actually changes, not on every spurious
  AppKit-internal appearance churn.

Measured impact (idle, popover closed): 96% → 0.0% CPU; appearance KVO
short-circuit ratio at startup observed at 16/17 fires.

Entire-Checkpoint: 910295e4d170

* test(menubar): extract MenuBarRefreshController and cover dedup logic

The two dedup locks that broke the render feedback loop were inlined in
AppDelegate, where the AppKit dependencies (NSStatusItem, NSImage,
KVO on effectiveAppearance) make them awkward to test. Extract the
pure dedup state into `MenuBarRefreshController` in the lib target,
so the cache invariants can be asserted without standing up AppKit.

The controller exposes:
- `refresh(key:)` — invokes the render callback iff `key` differs from
  the last accepted render; the call that propagates back through KVO
  becomes a no-op and the feedback loop terminates.
- `shouldHandleAppearanceChange(isDark:)` — lets the appearance KVO
  observer filter fires that do not move the resolved dark/aqua flag.
- `renderCount` — exposed for the upcoming integration smoke test.

Tests cover the contract that prevents the regression:
- First refresh always renders exactly once.
- 1000 identical refreshes coalesce to one render.
- Each of the five MenuBarRenderKey fields independently invalidates
  the cache (one test per field, so a future field addition that
  forgets to participate in equality fails locally).
- Appearance fires with unchanged isDark are reported as no-op;
  actual flips are authorised then re-filtered.

Entire-Checkpoint: 1d1a147667cd

* test(menubar): add opt-in smoke test for the render feedback loop

The dedup unit tests cover the controller's contract in isolation, but
they cannot reproduce the actual NSStatusItem ↔ effectiveAppearance KVO
feedback that caused the original CPU spike — that requires a real
AppKit process with a graphical session. This commit adds an
integration smoke test that:

- launches the compiled AIUsagesTrackers binary,
- sandboxes its cache via AI_TRACKER_CACHE_DIR (so the user's
  ~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker is untouched),
- reads the menu bar render count flushed every 2 s by the running app
  (new AI_TRACKER_EXPORT_RENDER_COUNT mechanism),
- asserts the count stays below 100 over an 8 s idle window.

Before the fix the same observation window yielded ~4800 renders
(600/s × 8 s). I verified the test discriminates correctly by
temporarily removing both dedup locks: count jumped to 4299, the test
failed as expected.

The test is opt-in (RUN_SMOKE_TESTS=1) because it:
- spawns a real AppKit process (~10 s wall time, intrusive),
- needs a graphical session for NSStatusBar,
- may be inconsistent on headless CI runners where the
  NSStatusItemReplicantShadowView shadowing machinery doesn't fire.

The binary path is taken from AIUT_BINARY_PATH if set, otherwise from
`.build/<triple>/{debug,release}/AIUsagesTrackers` relative to cwd, so
local developers can run it with `swift build && RUN_SMOKE_TESTS=1
swift test --filter StatusItemRenderSmoke`.

Two small support changes in the lib:
- `Loggers.cacheDir` becomes public and honours AI_TRACKER_CACHE_DIR
  so the test can divert all on-disk state to a temp dir.
- `AppPidGuard` is initialised from `Loggers.cacheDir` instead of
  recomputing the path from `$HOME`, so the sandbox applies to the
  pid file too.

447aad8 fix(menubar): use single source of truth for dot color when percent unknown (#38)
When a time-window metric's percent is unknown (resetAt missing/elapsed,
thus tier == nil), the dot color previously fell back to the surrounding
text color. This caused inconsistency between the real menu bar and the
Settings preview when they differed in appearance (e.g. dark menu bar vs
light Settings window).

Solution: add Optional<ConsumptionTier>.dotNSColor as the single source of
truth. Unknown case maps to a fixed mid-gray (not appearance-adaptive),
ensuring consistent visuals across surfaces. Add a contrasted stroke around
the dot (text color) for visibility against menu-bar wallpapers that happen
to match the fill color.

The fill is now data-driven (segment-identity); the stroke is context-driven
(for legibility only).

All tests pass.

447aad8 fix(menubar): use single source of truth for dot color when percent unknown (#38)
When a time-window metric's percent is unknown (resetAt missing/elapsed,
thus tier == nil), the dot color previously fell back to the surrounding
text color. This caused inconsistency between the real menu bar and the
Settings preview when they differed in appearance (e.g. dark menu bar vs
light Settings window).

Solution: add Optional<ConsumptionTier>.dotNSColor as the single source of
truth. Unknown case maps to a fixed mid-gray (not appearance-adaptive),
ensuring consistent visuals across surfaces. Add a contrasted stroke around
the dot (text color) for visibility against menu-bar wallpapers that happen
to match the fill color.

The fill is now data-driven (segment-identity); the stroke is context-driven
(for legibility only).
